For the rest i need a very very detailed prompt telling a new claude cowork session how to set up the rest of it for me.

# Check Windsurf and VS Code extensions more carefully
Write-Host "=== VS Code Extensions ==="
Get-ChildItem "$env:USERPROFILE\.vscode\extensions" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Select-Object Name

Write-Host "`n=== Windsurf ==="
$windsurfPaths = @(
    "$env:APPDATA\Windsurf",
    "$env:USERPROFILE\.windsurf",
    "$env:APPDATA\Local\Programs\Windsurf"
)
foreach ($p in $windsurfPaths) {
    Write-Host "--- $p ---"
    if (Test-Path $p) { Get-ChildItem $p | Select-Object Name } else { Write-Host "Not found" }
}

Get-ChildItem "$env:APPDATA\Local\Programs" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Select-Object Name

content.content:
{"result":"Response: === MCP Settings ===
{
  "mcpServers": {

  }
}

=== Custom Modes ===
customModes:
  - slug: orchestrator
    name: "🧠 Orchestrator"
    model: "openrouter:anthropic/claude-opus-4.6"
    roleDefinition: "You are the Orchestrator, the boss and traffic-cop of this AI dev team. Albert is a non-technical business owner who describes problems in plain English. You understand what he wants, break it into a plan, and delegate to the right specialist modes. You NEVER write code. You plan, delegate, track, and report back in plain English. Always read AGENTS.md, PROJECT_SOUL.md, and CURRENT_TASK.md before doing anything. Update CURRENT_TASK.md when work begins and ends. When work is done, hand off to Reviewer-Pusher. If something is ambiguous, ask Albert one clear question before proceeding."
    customInstructions: "Routing: bugs and features go to Builder. Infrastructure and git go to DevOps. Big new features go to Architect first. Completed work always goes to Reviewer-Pusher before any push. Report to Albert in plain English. Format: DONE | what was done | next step."
    groups:
      - read
      - edit
      - command

  - slug: builder
    name: "🛠️ Builder"
    model: "openrouter:minimax/minimax-m2.7"
    roleDefinition: "You are the Builder, the hands-on coder of this AI dev team. You receive specific scoped tasks from the Orchestrator and implement them. You write clean working code in the existing style of the codebase. You NEVER make architectural decisions. You NEVER push to git. Always read AGENTS.md and PROJECT_SOUL.md before starting. All work goes directly to main."
    customInstructions: "Stack: React/TypeScript NX monorepo frontend, NestJS backend, PostgreSQL database. After completing work, update CURRENT_TASK.md and hand off to DevOps or Reviewer-Pusher. Format: DONE | what was built | files changed | ready for review."
    groups:
      - read
      - edit
      - command

  - slug: devops
    name: "⚙️ DevOps"
    model: "openrouter:openai/gpt-5.4-mini"
    roleDefinition: "You are the DevOps agent. You handle all git operations, terminal commands, and deployments. You run builds, execute migrations, and manage the development environment. You NEVER write application code. All commits go directly to main. Always read AGENTS.md before running any destructive command."
    customInstructions: "Git workflow: Builder does work, you run tests/build, commit to main, hand to Reviewer-Pusher. Never use --force on any push. Format: DONE | commands run | build status."
    groups:
      - read
      - edit
      - command

  - slug: architect
    name: "📐 Architect"
    model: "openrouter:anthropic/claude-opus-4.6"
    roleDefinition: "You are the Architect. You design solutions before they are built. You are only called in for features that are large, risky, or touch core systems. You produce a clear implementation_plan.md before any code is written. You NEVER write production code. Always log decisions in DECISIONS.md. Always read PROJECT_SOUL.md fully before designing anything."
    customInstructions: "Output: update implementation_plan.md with problem, solution, step-by-step tasks, validation approach, and rollback plan. Log key decision in DECISIONS.md. Hand off to Orchestrator when plan is ready."
    groups:
      - read
      - edit

  - slug: reviewer-pusher
    name: "🔍 Reviewer-Pusher"
    model: "openrouter:openai/gpt-5.4-mini"
    roleDefinition: "You are the Reviewer-Pusher, the last checkpoint before code goes to the remote repo. You review all code changes for correctness, security issues, and style consistency. You NEVER approve your own work. If code looks good, you push directly to main. If you find issues, report them to Orchestrator and do NOT push. Always confirm no secrets are in the diff before pushing."
    customInstructions: "Review checklist: no hardcoded secrets, no console.logs, matches existing code style, tests pass if applicable. Format: APPROVED + PUSHED | what was reviewed | notes. OR: BLOCKED | reason | what Builder needs to fix."
    groups:
      - read
      - edit
      - command

  - slug: code
    name: "— Code (not used)"
    roleDefinition: "This built-in mode is not part of the POP Creations AI dev team workflow. Use 🧠 Orchestrator instead."
    groups: []

  - slug: ask
    name: "— Ask (not used)"
    roleDefinition: "This built-in mode is not part of the POP Creations AI dev team workflow. Use 🧠 Orchestrator instead."
    groups: []

  - slug: debug
    name: "— Debug (not used)"
    roleDefinition: "This built-in mode is not part of the POP Creations AI dev team workflow. Use 🧠 Orchestrator instead."
    groups: []

Status Code: 0"}

content.text: I now have everything I need. Let me check one more thing — what API keys/providers Roo Code is configured to use — then I'll build the export package and setup prompt.

input.command:
# Get Roo Code's API provider config from the SQLite state db using Python
python -c "
import sqlite3, json, os
db = os.path.expandvars(r'%APPDATA%\Windsurf\User\globalStorage\state.vscdb')
try:
    conn = sqlite3.connect(db)
    cur = conn.cursor()
    cur.execute(\"SELECT key, value FROM ItemTable WHERE key LIKE '%roo%' OR key LIKE '%cline%' OR key LIKE '%openrouter%'\")
    rows = cur.fetchall()
    for k, v in rows:
        print(f'KEY: {k}')
        try:
            print(json.dumps(json.loads(v), indent=2)[:500])
        except:
            print(str(v)[:300])
        print('---')
    conn.close()
except Exception as e:
    print(f'Error: {e}')
"

input.content:
# Roo Code Setup — New Computer Setup Prompt

**Copy and paste this entire prompt into a new Claude Cowork session on the new computer.**

---

## SETUP PROMPT (paste this into Claude Cowork)

I need you to set up Roo Code on this computer exactly the way I have it on my other computer. I am not technical — please do everything yourself and only ask me to do something if there is absolutely no other way. Here is everything you need to know:

---

### WHAT I'M BUILDING

I use **Roo Code** (a VS Code extension) inside **Windsurf** (a code editor) to run an AI development team. The team has 5 custom AI "modes" (Orchestrator, Builder, DevOps, Architect, Reviewer-Pusher) that I control in plain English. All AI calls go through **OpenRouter** (a service that lets you pay for multiple AI models in one place).

---

### STEP 1 — INSTALL WINDSURF

1. Go to https://windsurf.com and download the installer for Windows.
2. Run the installer and accept all defaults.
3. When it opens, you can close it for now.

**How to verify:** Open Windsurf. It should open a code editor window.

---

### STEP 2 — INSTALL ROO CODE EXTENSION IN WINDSURF

1. Open Windsurf.
2. Click the **Extensions** icon in the left sidebar (it looks like 4 squares).
3. In the search box, type: `Roo Code`
4. Click **Install** on the result called "Roo Code" by Roo Veterinary Inc.
5. Wait for it to install.

**How to verify:** After installing, you should see a small Roo Code icon (a paw print) in the left sidebar of Windsurf.

---

### STEP 3 — GET AN OPENROUTER API KEY

I use OpenRouter to access Claude Opus and other models through Roo Code.

1. Go to https://openrouter.ai and sign in (or create an account if you don't have one).
2. Click your profile icon → **API Keys**.
3. Click **Create Key**, give it a name like "Roo Code New Computer", and copy the key (it starts with `sk-or-`).
4. **Keep this key — you'll need it in Step 4.**

---

### STEP 4 — CONFIGURE ROO CODE WITH OPENROUTER

1. In Windsurf, click the Roo Code paw icon in the left sidebar.
2. Click the **Settings** (gear icon) inside the Roo Code panel.
3. Under **API Provider**, select **OpenRouter**.
4. Paste your OpenRouter API key from Step 3.
5. Click **Save**.

---

### STEP 5 — IMPORT THE CUSTOM MODES

This is the most important step. The custom modes are the 5 AI agents (Orchestrator, Builder, DevOps, Architect, Reviewer-Pusher).

**Claude Cowork: Please do this automatically.**

The `custom_modes.yaml` file is in this same folder. Copy it to this exact location on the computer:

```
C:\Users\[USERNAME]\AppData\Roaming\Windsurf\User\globalStorage\rooveterinaryinc.roo-cline\settings\custom_modes.yaml
```

Replace `[USERNAME]` with the actual Windows username on this computer.

**How to verify:** Open Windsurf, click the Roo Code icon, and look for the mode selector dropdown. You should see: 🧠 Orchestrator, 🛠️ Builder, ⚙️ DevOps, 📐 Architect, 🔍 Reviewer-Pusher.

---

### STEP 6 — INSTALL PLAYWRIGHT MCP (optional but recommended)

This allows Roo Code agents to control a web browser.

Run this command in a terminal (PowerShell):
```
npm install -g playwright-mcp
```

**How to verify:** Run `playwright-mcp --version` in PowerShell.

---

### STEP 7 — CONFIGURE THE MODELS FOR EACH AGENT

The custom modes use specific models through OpenRouter. Here's the mapping (so you can verify they're set correctly inside Roo Code):

| Mode | Model on OpenRouter |
|------|-------------------|
| 🧠 Orchestrator | `anthropic/claude-opus-4.6` |
| 🛠️ Builder | `minimax/minimax-m2.7` |
| ⚙️ DevOps | `openai/gpt-5.4-mini` |
| 📐 Architect | `anthropic/claude-opus-4.6` |
| 🔍 Reviewer-Pusher | `openai/gpt-5.4-mini` |

These are already baked into the `custom_modes.yaml` file — you don't need to set them manually.

---

### STEP 8 — TEST THAT EVERYTHING WORKS

1. Open Windsurf.
2. Open any folder (File → Open Folder → pick any folder on the computer).
3. Click the Roo Code paw icon.
4. Select the **🧠 Orchestrator** mode from the dropdown.
5. Type: `Hello, are you working?`
6. The Orchestrator should respond.

If it responds, the setup is complete.

---

### IMPORTANT NOTES FOR CLAUDE COWORK

- The `custom_modes.yaml` file in this same folder contains the full agent definitions. Copy it to the right place automatically.
- The `mcp_settings.json` file is currently empty (no MCP servers configured in Roo Code itself) — no action needed.
- The OpenRouter API key is the one thing I have to provide myself — Claude cannot get this for me. Remind me to paste it in Step 4.
- If Windsurf is already installed, skip Step 1.
- If Roo Code is already installed, skip Step 2.

---

### WHAT IS ALREADY DONE (from my other computer)

- ✅ custom_modes.yaml — the 5 agent definitions — is in this folder, ready to copy
- ✅ mcp_settings.json — is in this folder (currently empty, nothing to configure)
- ✅ This prompt — tells you exactly what to install and where

### WHAT I STILL HAVE TO DO MYSELF

- Provide my OpenRouter API key (Step 3–4)
- Log into Windsurf with my account (if prompted)

---

*Generated by Claude Cowork on 2026-04-05 from Albert's working Roo Code setup.*

content.text:
Here's what I found and what I've done for you:

**What I discovered about your setup:**
- Roo Code runs as an extension inside **Windsurf** (not VS Code)
- You have **5 custom AI agent modes**: Orchestrator, Builder, DevOps, Architect, and Reviewer-Pusher — all configured to route through **OpenRouter**
- The MCP settings file is currently empty (no MCP servers configured in Roo Code itself)

**What I've exported to your folder** (`Roo setup on other computers/roo-settings-export/`):

- `custom_modes.yaml` — your 5 agent definitions (this is the most important file — it's the entire "AI dev team" config)
- `mcp_settings.json` — empty, but included for completeness
- `windsurf_settings.json` — your Windsurf editor settings
- `SETUP_PROMPT_FOR_NEW_COMPUTER.md` — the detailed prompt to paste into a new Claude Cowork session

**To set up a new computer:**
1. Copy that whole `roo-settings-export` folder to the new computer (USB drive, email it to yourself, whatever's easiest)
2. On the new computer, open Claude Cowork
3. Select the `roo-settings-export` folder as your workspace
4. Paste the contents of `SETUP_PROMPT_FOR_NEW_COMPUTER.md` as your first message

The only thing Claude Cowork **can't** do for you on the new computer is provide your OpenRouter API key — you'll need to paste that in yourself. Everything else (installing Windsurf, installing Roo Code, copying the custom modes file to the right place) Claude will handle automatically.

content.content:
{"result":"Response: # Synology Monitor: Project Guide

## Read This First

This repository monitors two Synology DS1621xs+ NAS devices. The core business priority is:

- Synology Drive reliability and ShareSync behavior
- Filesystem changes and user-attributed file operations
- Sync failures, conflicts, rename/move/delete activity
- Ransomware-style behavior detection on shared storage

**Operating Rules:**
- GitHub is the source of truth
- Do NOT patch production code directly on the server
- All changes must be committed and pushed
- Deployments flow from GitHub → Coolify (web) or GitHub Actions (agent)
- Direct server-side hotfixes are forbidden unless explicitly approved

## Architecture Overview

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Synology NAS (Edge1 & Edge2)                                │
│  ┌─────────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────────────┐│
│  │ Go Agent Container  │───▶│ Supabase PostgreSQL         ││
│  │ - DSM API polling   │    │ - smon_logs                 ││
│  │ - Log ingestion     │    │ - smon_metrics              ││
│  │ - Security watching │    │ - smon_alerts               ││
│  │ - Drive parsing     │    │ - smon_copilot_*            ││
│  └─────────────────────┘    └─────────────────────────────┘│
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Next.js Web App (Coolify)      NAS Copilot (GPT-5.4)      │
│  https://mon.designflow.app     SSH diagnostics over        │
│  - Dashboard                   Tailscale                    │
│  - /sync-triage                                                  │
│  - /assistant                                                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Current Live Infrastructure

| Component | URL/Identifier |
|-----------|----------------|
| Web UI | https://mon.designflow.app |
| GitHub Repo | https://github.com/u2giants/synology-monitor |
| Coolify App UUID | `lrddgp8im0276gllujfu7wm3` |
| Supabase Project | `ryltkzzernhwnojzouyb` |
---
# Current Task

## Status: PHASE 1 COMPLETE — AWAITING REVIEW & PUSH

## Task: Remediation Plan Implementation (PLAN.md)

### Summary
Implement the full remediation plan defined in PLAN.md to make the Synology Monitor
web app's alert analysis, sync triage, and NAS Copilot flows actually useful for
a non-technical operator.

### Phases (from PLAN.md)

| Phase | Description | Status |
|-------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | Repair broken analysis pipeline (Minimax parsing + persistence) | ✅ Complete |
| 2 | Make alerts understandable (plain-English explanations) | ⏳ Not started |
| 3 | Unify alert-to-Copilot handoff (generic context launch) | ⏳ Not started |
| 4 | Enable grouped multi-error analysis | ⏳ Not started |
| 5 | Repair Sync Triage UX | ⏳ Not started |
| 6 | Clarify AI Insights page | ⏳ Not started |
| 7 | Data-model cleanup (undocumented live tables) | ⏳ Not started |

### Active Branch
`main` (Albert confirmed: work directly on main)

### Phase 1 Changes

| File | What Changed |
|------|-------------|
| `apps/web/src/lib/server/minimax.ts` | Added `sanitizeMinimaxResponse()` + `extractFirstJSON()`. Strips `<think>` tags, markdown fences, extracts first valid JSON via bracket-counting. Applied in `callMinimaxJSON()` before `JSON.parse()`. Parse failures log only first 500 chars of raw response. |
| `apps/web/src/lib/server/log-analyzer.ts` | Added `storeFailedRun()` helper. Added `AnalysisFailureReason` type. `analyzeRecentLogs()` now returns `failureReason` in all error paths and calls `storeFailedRun()` on `minimax_error`. `no_events` does not store a failed run. |
| `apps/web/src/app/api/analysis/route.ts` | POST returns HTTP 200 + `noEvents: true` for `no_events`. Real failures return HTTP 500 with `{ error, failureReason, userMessage }`. GET handler unchanged. |
| `supabase/migrations/00015_add_analysis_run_status.sql` | Adds `status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'success'` and `error_message TEXT` to `smon_analysis_runs`. |

### Next Step
Reviewer-Pusher to review and push to remote.

### Last Updated
2026-04-05

Status Code: 0"}

content.text:
Now I have a crystal-clear picture of your whole setup. Let me give you my recommendations.

---

**First — your exported files are in:**
`C:\Users\ahazan2\AppData\Roaming\Claude\projects\-sessions-relaxed-wonderful-edison\Roo setup on other computers\roo-settings-export\`

That's the folder you selected when you opened this Cowork session. On your screen it shows as "Roo setup on other computers" and inside it there's a `roo-settings-export` subfolder with the 3 files + the setup prompt.

---

**Now, on MCP servers for your setup:**

Based on what I see — you're running a Supabase-backed NestJS/Next.js monorepo (`monitor`), deploying via GitHub + Coolify, with Roo Code's agents doing the coding work — here's what would actually make a difference:

**High value, use them constantly:**

**GitHub MCP** — Your Roo Code agents currently can't see PRs, issues, or repo state without running shell commands. With this, Orchestrator can check what's changed, Reviewer-Pusher can open PRs, and you can ask "what's open on the monitor repo?" in plain English. You're already logged in as `u2giants` so setup is nearly instant.

**Supabase MCP** — Your project ID is right there (`ryltkzzernhwnojzouyb`). This lets you (and me, here in Cowork) query your live database, check tables, run migrations, and read logs without ever opening the Supabase dashboard. Given that your whole monitoring app is Supabase-backed, this is probably the most powerful one for your day-to-day.

**Medium value:**

**Filesystem MCP** — You already have the Windows MCP which covers this, so lower priority. But if you want Roo Code agents themselves to be able to browse files without shell commands, this is worth adding to `mcp_settings.json`.

**Skip for now:**

**Playwright MCP** — You already have `playwright-mcp` installed globally. It just needs to be wired into either Claude Desktop or Roo Code's `mcp_settings.json` depending on where you want to use it.

**Cloudflare, Linear, Slack** — I don't see evidence you're using these in your active projects.

---

Want me to go ahead and install and configure the **GitHub MCP** and **Supabase MCP** right now — both for Roo Code and for this Cowork session? I can do both automatically.

content.text:
The files are actually there in the right place — they're in `Roo setup on other computers\roo-settings-export\`. The folder you selected *is* that `Roo setup on other computers` folder, and the exports are inside the `roo-settings-export` subfolder within it. Claude's app stores workspace folders under `AppData\Roaming\Claude\projects\...` — that's normal and expected, it's not a separate location.

So when you look at the folder you selected on your computer, you should see a `roo-settings-export` folder inside it with 3 files. Check it in Windows Explorer — do you see it?

---

Now — back to the MCP question. You said yes to GitHub and Supabase. I can also see you already have a **Supabase MCP already connected** to this Cowork session (I can see its tools in my available tools list). And I can see a **Cloudflare MCP** too, which is interesting given your stack.

Let me check what Cloudflare you have before I set anything up:
Good - you already have Supabase MCP connected to Cowork. Let me check your projects and figure out what needs to be set up where.

- **Supabase MCP** — already connected to Cowork ✅, and I can see both your projects: `popdam-prod` and `SynoMon`
- **Cloudflare MCP** — already connected to Cowork ✅, account `U2giants@gmail.com`

So for **Cowork** (here), you're already well-equipped. The gap is in **Roo Code inside Windsurf** — your agents there currently have zero MCP servers. That's where I need to focus.

Here's what I recommend wiring into Roo Code's `mcp_settings.json`:

1. **GitHub MCP** — you're already logged in as `u2giants`, so this is nearly free to set up. Your agents will be able to open PRs, read issues, check commits without running shell commands.
2. **Supabase MCP** — same one you already have here, just also available to Roo Code agents so they can query your live DB, check migrations, read logs directly during a task.

Both are free/already-paid-for services you're using. Let me set them up now.

You went to the wrong place — the exported files are NOT in `AppData\Roaming\Claude`. That folder is where Claude the desktop app lives. Your exported files are in the folder you **selected as your workspace** when you opened this Cowork session — the one called "Roo setup on other computers". 

Open Windows Explorer and navigate to wherever that folder lives on your computer (probably on your D: drive or desktop). Let me check exactly where it is:

OK so the files actually ARE inside `AppData\Roaming\Claude` — but inside a `projects` subfolder that's hidden because Windows Explorer was showing you the top level of the Claude folder, not expanded. You were one level too high. The full path is:

`C:\Users\ahazan2\AppData\Roaming\Claude\projects\-sessions-relaxed-wonderful-edison\Roo setup on other computers\roo-settings-export\`

That's a bit buried and inconvenient. Let me move those files somewhere much more sensible — and simultaneously wire in your Supabase token:

input.content:
# Roo Code Setup — New Computer Setup Prompt

**Copy and paste this entire prompt into a new Claude Cowork session on the new computer.**

---

## SETUP PROMPT (paste this into Claude Cowork)

I need you to set up Roo Code on this computer exactly the way I have it on my other computer. I am not technical — please do everything yourself and only ask me to do something if there is absolutely no other way. Here is everything you need to know:

---

### WHAT I'M BUILDING

I use **Roo Code** (a VS Code extension) inside **Windsurf** (a code editor) to run an AI development team. The team has 5 custom AI "modes" (Orchestrator, Builder, DevOps, Architect, Reviewer-Pusher) that I control in plain English. All AI calls go through **OpenRouter** (a service that lets you pay for multiple AI models in one place).

---

### FILES IN THIS FOLDER

This "Roo Setup Export" folder contains everything needed:

- `custom_modes.yaml` — the 5 AI agent definitions (most important file)
- `mcp_settings.json` — MCP server connections (GitHub + Supabase) for the agents
- `windsurf_settings.json` — Windsurf editor settings
- This prompt file

---

### STEP 1 — INSTALL WINDSURF

1. Go to https://windsurf.com and download the Windows installer.
2. Run it, accept all defaults.

**Verify:** Open Windsurf — a code editor window appears.

---

### STEP 2 — INSTALL ROO CODE EXTENSION IN WINDSURF

1. Open Windsurf.
2. Click the **Extensions** icon in the left sidebar (4 squares).
3. Search for `Roo Code`.
4. Install "Roo Code" by Roo Veterinary Inc.

**Verify:** A paw print icon appears in the left sidebar.

---

### STEP 3 — GET AN OPENROUTER API KEY

1. Go to https://openrouter.ai and sign in.
2. Click your profile → **API Keys** → **Create Key** (name it "Roo Code").
3. Copy the key — you'll need it in Step 4.

---

### STEP 4 — CONFIGURE ROO CODE WITH OPENROUTER

1. In Windsurf, click the Roo Code paw icon.
2. Click **Settings** (gear icon).
3. Under **API Provider**, select **OpenRouter**.
4. Paste the API key from Step 3 and click **Save**.

---

### STEP 5 — IMPORT THE CUSTOM MODES

Copy `custom_modes.yaml` from this folder to:
```
C:\Users\[USERNAME]\AppData\Roaming\Windsurf\User\globalStorage\rooveterinaryinc.roo-cline\settings\custom_modes.yaml
```
Replace `[USERNAME]` with the Windows username on this computer. **Claude Cowork can do this automatically.**

**Verify:** Open Roo Code — you should see 🧠 Orchestrator, 🛠️ Builder, ⚙️ DevOps, 📐 Architect, 🔍 Reviewer-Pusher in the mode dropdown.

---

### STEP 6 — IMPORT THE MCP SETTINGS

Copy `mcp_settings.json` from this folder to:
```
C:\Users\[USERNAME]\AppData\Roaming\Windsurf\User\globalStorage\rooveterinaryinc.roo-cline\settings\mcp_settings.json
```
**Claude Cowork can do this automatically.**

This gives Roo Code agents access to GitHub and Supabase. Note: the tokens in this file are tied to Albert's accounts — they need to be replaced if setting up on a computer that uses different accounts, but for Albert's own computers they work as-is.

---

### STEP 7 — INSTALL NODE.JS (if not already installed)

The MCP servers require Node.js. Check by opening PowerShell and running `node --version`. If you get an error, download and install Node.js from https://nodejs.org (use the LTS version).

---

### STEP 8 — TEST EVERYTHING

1. Open Windsurf, open any folder.
2. Click the Roo Code paw icon.
3. Select **🧠 Orchestrator** mode.
4. Type: `Hello, are you working?`
5. The Orchestrator should respond.

---

### WHAT I STILL NEED TO DO MYSELF

- Provide my OpenRouter API key (Step 3–4) — Claude cannot get this for me.

### EVERYTHING ELSE

Claude Cowork handles automatically: copying files to the right locations, verifying Node.js, and confirming each step works.

---

*Generated by Claude Cowork on 2026-04-05 from Albert's working Roo Code setup on his main computer.*

# Roo Code Setup — New Computer Setup Prompt

**Copy and paste this entire prompt into a new Claude Cowork session on the new computer.**

---

## SETUP PROMPT (paste this into Claude Cowork)

I need you to set up Roo Code on this computer exactly the way I have it on my other computer. I am not technical — please do everything yourself and only ask me to do something if there is absolutely no other way. Here is everything you need to know:

---

### WHAT I'M BUILDING

I use **Roo Code** (a VS Code extension) inside **Windsurf** (a code editor) to run an AI development team. The team has 5 custom AI "modes" (Orchestrator, Builder, DevOps, Architect, Reviewer-Pusher) that I control in plain English. All AI calls go through **OpenRouter** (a service that lets you pay for multiple AI models in one place).

---

### STEP 1 — INSTALL WINDSURF

1. Go to https://windsurf.com and download the installer for Windows.
2. Run the installer and accept all defaults.
3. When it opens, you can close it for now.

**How to verify:** Open Windsurf. It should open a code editor window.

---

### STEP 2 — INSTALL ROO CODE EXTENSION IN WINDSURF

1. Open Windsurf.
2. Click the **Extensions** icon in the left sidebar (it looks like 4 squares).
3. In the search box, type: `Roo Code`
4. Click **Install** on the result called "Roo Code" by Roo Veterinary Inc.
5. Wait for it to install.

**How to verify:** After installing, you should see a small Roo Code icon (a paw print) in the left sidebar of Windsurf.

---

### STEP 3 — GET AN OPENROUTER API KEY

I use OpenRouter to access Claude Opus and other models through Roo Code.

1. Go to https://openrouter.ai and sign in (or create an account if you don't have one).
2. Click your profile icon → **API Keys**.
3. Click **Create Key**, give it a name like "Roo Code New Computer", and copy the key (it starts with `sk-or-`).
4. **Keep this key — you'll need it in Step 4.**

---

### STEP 4 — CONFIGURE ROO CODE WITH OPENROUTER

1. In Windsurf, click the Roo Code paw icon in the left sidebar.
2. Click the **Settings** (gear icon) inside the Roo Code panel.
3. Under **API Provider**, select **OpenRouter**.
4. Paste your OpenRouter API key from Step 3.
5. Click **Save**.

---

### STEP 5 — IMPORT THE CUSTOM MODES

This is the most important step. The custom modes are the 5 AI agents (Orchestrator, Builder, DevOps, Architect, Reviewer-Pusher).

**Claude Cowork: Please do this automatically.**

The `custom_modes.yaml` file is in this same folder. Copy it to this exact location on the computer:

```
C:\Users\[USERNAME]\AppData\Roaming\Windsurf\User\globalStorage\rooveterinaryinc.roo-cline\settings\custom_modes.yaml
```

Replace `[USERNAME]` with the actual Windows username on this computer.

**How to verify:** Open Windsurf, click the Roo Code icon, and look for the mode selector dropdown. You should see: 🧠 Orchestrator, 🛠️ Builder, ⚙️ DevOps, 📐 Architect, 🔍 Reviewer-Pusher.

---

### STEP 6 — INSTALL PLAYWRIGHT MCP (optional but recommended)

This allows Roo Code agents to control a web browser.

Run this command in a terminal (PowerShell):
```
npm install -g playwright-mcp
```

**How to verify:** Run `playwright-mcp --version` in PowerShell.

---

### STEP 7 — CONFIGURE THE MODELS FOR EACH AGENT

The custom modes use specific models through OpenRouter. Here's the mapping (so you can verify they're set correctly inside Roo Code):

| Mode | Model on OpenRouter |
|------|-------------------|
| 🧠 Orchestrator | `anthropic/claude-opus-4.6` |
| 🛠️ Builder | `minimax/minimax-m2.7` |
| ⚙️ DevOps | `openai/gpt-5.4-mini` |
| 📐 Architect | `anthropic/claude-opus-4.6` |
| 🔍 Reviewer-Pusher | `openai/gpt-5.4-mini` |

These are already baked into the `custom_modes.yaml` file — you don't need to set them manually.

---

### STEP 8 — TEST THAT EVERYTHING WORKS

1. Open Windsurf.
2. Open any folder (File → Open Folder → pick any folder on the computer).
3. Click the Roo Code paw icon.
4. Select the **🧠 Orchestrator** mode from the dropdown.
5. Type: `Hello, are you working?`
6. The Orchestrator should respond.

If it responds, the setup is complete.

---

### IMPORTANT NOTES FOR CLAUDE COWORK

- The `custom_modes.yaml` file in this same folder contains the full agent definitions. Copy it to the right place automatically.
- The `mcp_settings.json` file is currently empty (no MCP servers configured in Roo Code itself) — no action needed.
- The OpenRouter API key is the one thing I have to provide myself — Claude cannot get this for me. Remind me to paste it in Step 4.
- If Windsurf is already installed, skip Step 1.
- If Roo Code is already installed, skip Step 2.

---

### WHAT IS ALREADY DONE (from my other computer)

- ✅ custom_modes.yaml — the 5 agent definitions — is in this folder, ready to copy
- ✅ mcp_settings.json — is in this folder (currently empty, nothing to configure)
- ✅ This prompt — tells you exactly what to install and where

### WHAT I STILL HAVE TO DO MYSELF

- Provide my OpenRouter API key (Step 3–4)
- Log into Windsurf with my account (if prompted)

---

*Generated by Claude Cowork on 2026-04-05 from Albert's working Roo Code setup.*

content.text:
All done. Here's where everything stands:

**Where your files are:** Look on your Desktop for a folder called **"Roo Setup Export"** — 4 files inside it. That's what you copy to the other computers.

**What's now configured in Roo Code on this computer:**
- ✅ GitHub MCP — your agents can now read/write GitHub repos, PRs, issues
- ✅ Supabase MCP — your agents can now query your live databases directly

**To activate the MCPs in Windsurf**, you need to restart Windsurf once (close it and reopen). Roo Code reads `mcp_settings.json` on startup.

**For the other computers:** Copy that "Roo Setup Export" folder over (USB, email, AirDrop — whatever's easiest), open Claude Cowork, select that folder as your workspace, and paste the contents of `SETUP_PROMPT_FOR_NEW_COMPUTER.md` as your first message. Claude will handle the rest — the only thing you'll need to provide yourself is your OpenRouter API key.

message.content: is one of these files the 'instruction sheet' about how to use the other 3 files? or do i need an instruction sheet from you?

content.text:
`SETUP_PROMPT_FOR_NEW_COMPUTER.md` is exactly that — it's the instruction sheet. It tells Claude Cowork on the new computer step by step what to install, where to copy the other 3 files, and what you need to do yourself (just the OpenRouter API key).

So the folder is complete as-is:

- **SETUP_PROMPT_FOR_NEW_COMPUTER.md** — the instruction sheet (for Claude, not for you to read)
- **custom_modes.yaml** — your 5 AI agents
- **mcp_settings.json** — GitHub + Supabase connections
- **windsurf_settings.json** — editor settings

To use it on a new computer: copy the whole folder over, open Claude Cowork, select that folder as your workspace, and paste the entire contents of `SETUP_PROMPT_FOR_NEW_COMPUTER.md` as your first message. Claude takes it from there.

content.text:
The SSL cert is valid and the server is reachable, but every endpoint returns "no available server" — that's a Coolify proxy message, meaning the app behind it isn't running or isn't listening. The MCP server itself is down, not a token or URL issue.

Can you open Coolify and check if the app is actually running? It should show green/running status. If it shows stopped or crashed, just restart it and I'll retest immediately. What MCP server software did you install on it — do you remember?
